Hey y'all, let me explain something, 'cause someone's getting harassed right now because they made something that's "not a game."
The battleground you've drawn up in your head over the defintion of this term is one critical to the health & progress of games as a medium.
You understand this progress and the necessity of such definitions only as they apply to familiar cycles for interpreting art & information.
These cycles and the premise of "progress" within tech-driven arts are dictated entirely by the corporations that develop the hardware.
The SNES didn't die because all had been said with the hardware in the case. The SNES died because everyone had already bought one.
8-bit faded, 16-bit rose, 2D faded, 3D rose. Each transition point was hyped as a total revolution. I had an EGM subscription, just like you
Participating in this cycle of product releases made you feel you were on the bleeding edge, a wave that was carrying you into the future.
To feel that you were the most current, the most enlightened, the most informed was part of the marketing/experience of consuming games.
